figures.caption: Fig. 1: Comparison of the 2D marginal posterior distributions on the ξ1-ξ2 plane for the Rosenbrock inverse problem. Panel (a) shows the ground truth reference density, while panels (b)-(f) display the sampling results obtained by VF, VAE, MCMC, SVGD, and UKI, respectively.
Table 1: Relative inversion error eI of 1D Darcy Flow problem under different noise amplitudes and d values. Lower is better. Results are averaged over 3 runs per experiment.

Fig. 3: Qualitative comparison for the 1D Darcy Flow problem with d = 64 under different noise levels. For each noise level, the first row shows the reconstructed coefficient fields m and the second row shows the corresponding pressure fields p. From left to right: pCN, SVGD, UKI-FDM, UKI-FNO, and our proposed VF method.
Table 2: Relative inversion error eI of 2D Darcy Flow problem under different noise amplitudes and d values. Lower is better. Results are averaged over 3 runs per experiment.

Fig. 5: Qualitative comparison for the 2D Darcy Flow problem with d = 64 under different noise levels. For each noise level, the first row shows the reconstructed coefficient fields m and the second row shows the corresponding pressure fields p. From left to right: reference ground truth, pCN, SVGD, UKI-FDM, UKI-FNO, and our proposed VF method.

Fig. 6: Surrogate fitting error eS across adaptive stages of 2D Navier-Stokes problem. Columns: noise levels δ ∈ {1%, 5%, 10%}. Solid/dashed lines: d = 64/32. Overall, our VF method converges to lower errors in fewer stages.

Fig. 7: Qualitative comparison for the 2D Navier-Stokes problem with d = 64 under different noise levels. For each noise level, the first row shows the reconstructed initial vorticity fields ω0 and the second row shows the corresponding vorticity fields ω(·, T ) at T = 1. From left to right: reference ground truth, pCN, SVGD, UKI-FDM, UKIFNO, and our proposed VF method.
